Okay, welcome everybody. We're thrilled today to have John Carleton with us. And John, I'm so proud that you agreed to join us today. Oh, glad to be here. Always glad to chat with you. As I said before, we just never get a chance to, you know, to talk. I would prefer we get a whole call on war stories, but... That would be fun. Maybe we'll do it. For those of you who don't know John, I guess my first question would be what planet have you been living on? But let me give you just a little bit of background here. John's mentored under some of the greatest copywriters in the United States, including Jim Rottz, Jay Abraham, and Lake Erie Halbert. I first met John 18 years ago in a hotel lobby in Provo, Utah when he and Gary were sitting around chatting after a boot camp that we had just done. And then John and I pretty much lost contact with each other for years. But when we started the total package in 2005, John was one of the first people to write us and congratulate us and thank us for starting this whole package. And if any of you attended my Power Marketing Summit or bought the videos, you know John's insights and biting wit were a huge hit in our hot seat session. John was on our panel. He made you nervous, didn't he? No, you were fun. I mean, it was cool. Everybody was being so serious and John was just roasting these poor guys who were standing up there showing their direct mail pieces. I remember one in particular, the guy showing us his direct mail piece, and John says, well, the good news is that this crappy copy is working. You've got one hell of a product. That's really good news when you discover that's happening. Over the years, John's had a lot of hot controls for huge mailers like Rodale and Boardroom. Two of the packages that I've talked about quite often have been John's famous one-legged golfer promotion. And what was that headline again, John? The amazing secrets of a one-legged golfer can help you immediately launch longer drives. I think I'm specific. I don't have the actual headline. It's what you want for me to remember, Lane, especially now. Okay, and then Rodale's promotion for Sex, a man's guide, which mailed for five years. And these days, John is still writing, but he's also spending a lot of time helping the next generation of copywriters and direct marketers along through his big damn blog and his marketing rebel rant and several other websites. You can find John on the web at john-carleton.com and at marketingrebelrant.com. Actually, that would be marketingrebel.com, Clay. No, we're taking down the marketingrebelrant.com site. Oh, okay. All right. Good to know that. Yeah. I'm excited about today's webinar for a couple of reasons. In addition to the fact that I always enjoy spending time with John, one is that, as those of you who are on this call probably know, my background is, although I've written for a lot of different products, a lot of different markets, especially early in my career, for the last 15, 20 years or so, I've focused almost exclusively on the financial and the health markets. And while John has strong controls in those markets as well, he's done an awful lot of work in other markets like bodybuilding, self-defense, the golf market. And one of the first promos we'll be looking at today is a promo he wrote for a guitar course. And so John's insights reach into markets where mine really don't, at least in any recent experience. The second reason that I'm excited about this is that John's style is absolutely unique. He doesn't write like Gary Bencevenga or like me or like Jim Rutz or like many of the other people whose packages that you've seen. He has a very unique style. He has a very unique voice. And even though he's often called the web's most ripped-off copywriter, he still stands head and shoulders above most of the copywriters I see today. Essentially, and this is just my opinion, when I see John's copy, I see what Capels would be doing today if he was working in direct response. While other people have taken the, they laughed when I sat down to play the piano headline and ripped it off or altered it until hell won't have it, John has taken Capels' general approach of storytelling in order to engage and sell, and he's refined it to a fine art and moved it into the 21st century. And what is the long introduction, huh, John? I'm flushed. Thank you, and good night. My job is done here. I also want to point out that, well, most of the time we look at pieces that have been printed and mailed. Today John's given us kind of a special treat because we're actually looking at his manuscripts. This is the copy he writes just as he presents it to his clients. So again, John, thank you for joining us. And before we get into some of your promotions, I'd like to ask you just a little bit about your process. Okay. What are the things that you think about when you're beginning an assignment or preparing to begin? How do you get inside your prospect skin? Yeah, well, the first thing I think about is how to keep the money and get out of actually doing the job. I think that's the first step all veteran copywriters go through is. But that, of course, I get past that very quickly. I have a big part of my reputation when I started out was I literally was delivering copy overnight. I didn't know they would give me two weeks at the LA agencies when they'd sneak me in the back door and they'd get it the next day. So once you sat down and started writing copies, it was really simple stuff. But I've never missed a deadline in my career, and that used to piss off Jerry Halbert to no end. He said it made her bills look bad, and I should seriously consider missing a big deadline just to get back in the group with everyone else. So part of that thing, that process is, Clayton, I have to keep this PG rated, right? Well. One of the things that you have to realize as a writer is you really are a, I'm going to go ahead and say it because it's not offensive. We are professional prostitutes in a way. We have these major skills. We're not using them for our own products. We're using them for a client's product. And it's our job to get into the mindset to be that guy who essentially owns that company. That's where the gun to the head thing kind of starts. This whole attitude that Clayton, I know you don't call it that, but it's the same thing. And it's not a degradation type of thing. It's more of a just, okay, I am your man. Maybe a better way to do what you call the hired gun that you get in an army mercenary. It truly is a mercenary because you have to get so deep into the head of the client that you're writing for. Because part of the job of being a copywriter for another client is that he doesn't see the product the way he should. He usually sees it from the inside. So you have to take your expert ability to do sales detective work and wander in there and look at his product and fall in love with it as much as he is. Yet with that removed professionalism that allows you to deliver the benefits and find that hidden sales pitch and that hook or angle that's going to make it resonate with the people he's going after. So really the whole big part of the initial process is that kind of shaking off my own personality. I was only half-kidding about trying to get out of it because you have to love the process to be able to be successful as a copywriter. Yet still there is that, I guess it's like maybe Superman didn't always enjoy popping into the phone booth and changing clothes and going off to yet another thing. Maybe he had a date with Lois Lane coming up or something. It was an air fence. True pros just know regardless of whatever else is going on. You compartmentalize and you get into that state of the job becomes a puzzle to fix sometimes. The client becomes a mystery to solve. What is it about this client? What is it about this client? What he's doing that's not working now? Or how can I make whatever he has work to the target market he's after? When I started OutClaim, I don't know if you ever did this, but I used to actually have writing closed. I would put on a certain set of sweats and a hat. When I had that on, it served two purposes. One was that I couldn't leave the house because it was so filthy. I'd been wearing a bead sweats for so long and it was a dirty key shirt. So I was kind of trapped in the room by my own design. But when I put them on, I actually kind of pretended that there was a little bit of magical element to that. I am now the person who wears these clothes. My job is to sit here and to do the detective work and go deep and figure out what the heck is going on in this thing. It's never drudgery once you get into that, but just entering that state has to be a conscious decision. You can't be a good copywriter and say, well, I'll grab 15 minutes here and write a headline and then I'll come back after lunch and sit down and do that. You have to love the process enough to sit down and do your due diligence, which can be hours at a time, just since slogging away at this product that you didn't even know existed maybe a few days ago. And now you're drumming up total and unconditional love. I can really feel all of that. I didn't have the dirty hat and the sweats, but I had a very small room in my house that was a writing room. I think a lot of young writers kind of bite this, you know, you can write under a palm tree or sitting on the beach and all of this kind of stuff. And they take it hook, line, and sinker. But the fact is it requires that the kind of intensity you're talking about. I think the kind of focus that only comes from being in a more controlled environment, especially early on. You know, a lot of writers, and I recently have started taking advantage of having other writers help me write some pieces. Like they will sometimes, I'll send them off to do a little bit of research, so I'm not doing everything myself now. But when it comes down to the final draft, it's just you and the blank page. And you can't really write novels by committee, and you for sure can't write a good sales letter by committee. It just comes down to you. And there is a certain romance to it. It's almost like you're searching for the dragon hidden somewhere. When you get close and see smoke curling up from the mountain, you have to turn around and tell everyone else in the group to stay here. And then you wander off into the wilderness, and it's you and the dragon kind of. By conquering that stuff, we're damn proud of what we do. We've earned this ability. We've honed our chops. We discovered how to do it, but then we just kept doing it. And we didn't take things for granted. We were students for life. I still love reading Joe Sugarman's latest book, When I Sit Down with Other Guys Like You. When I talk about when to talk about war stories, it's also about what work, and you pick up a few things here and there. And it's just this general embracement of the process. You can't not like being in that isolated, it's you and the blank page, stage and succeed. I don't generally tell people that because people should self-select when they come off to be writers. One of the mistakes I think some of these other writing, I won't name names, but other organizations and such that claim to teach people how to write, they come at it from the wrong angle, which is the be your own boss kind of thing. And really, it's about you almost have to self-select because for me it's always been about a way to make money as a writer. How to turn your love of writing into a job that actually brings in an amazing amount of money and challenges you and keeps you fresh and your brain vibrant. I mean, there's all these benefits going on. But the soloing secrets was very important in this headline. If I had control of the board here, I would go and kind of outline the power words. And astonishing is not a power word there. The power words that went to the hook are soloing secrets, respected, ripped off, successful, lead guitarist, not just guitarist, and touring pro, clueless rookies, and then that last line, blow veteran guitarist clean off the stage. That's the money shot. I've written a dozen or so guitar ads. And I was the first guy in any of the guitar ads that I ever saw because I've been reading the Guitar Mag for 40 years. I was the first one to bring up the chicks will dig at angle. And I think I've got this. I might not have done it in this one. They may have actually asked me not to, but I'll bet. There. The fifth paragraph. You know when you hear the true guitar hero knows how to get inside your head with melody, inside your heart with feeling, and inside your soul with sexy, world-class musical mojo that's satisfied, bold, and gets the ladies wanting to boogie. So that's, you know, when you get a crowd dancing, when you're up there on stage, it's a visceral feeling of success no matter what else is going on. And it's often up to the rhythm section and the lead guitarist. And the lead guitarist knows it. You know, I wanted to make that point. It's fascinating. Whether you did it by sitting down and doing a left-brain inventory of benefits or not, you essentially went way beyond the product to what the dominant desires, the dominant emotions your marketplace was. And you covered respect, confidence, success, and getting the ladies' attention. And you did it all in a way that, you know, you didn't preach to them, look, you want respect, you want confidence, you know, you want the ladies. But they're almost throwaways. And so they keep people nodding, and they're totally on board with you all the way through this. I really love how you've done that. It's almost transparent to the person who's reading your copy, but it's causing something to happen at a much lower level, maybe in the reptilian brain. Well, that's a really good point too. I just love this line so much. If you've ever lusted for the ability to pick up any guitar in any situation, I don't know if you've sat in, but for anybody who has sat in and failed, they understand that when you get up there and the stage lights are in your eyes, and the other musicians, maybe the bass player kind of looks at your rolls his eyes, and you know, you have to prove your way. Anybody that's done that knows that feeling, and it can be very uncomfortable. And to be able to blow everybody off and have them be, you know, all you have to do is have some chops, and you will be accepted. And that is what, you know, that's one of the main things that, you know, even back at Capels and everybody else, when they list, you know, what it is you want, you know, it's love, and it's acceptance. It's like, you know, Clayton at your seminar, you had Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs, you know. It's one of the basic needs, that feeling of acceptance. And other guitar ads just don't quite make that connection. They take so much for granted. Of course you want to do that. Why would I mention that? Of course you want to be able to blow people off the stage. So why even talk about that? Well, because that's what your soul craze is. It's not just, you know, it's both the pretty picture of you doing it. You know, kind of seeing yourself up on stage, nervous, skeptical musicians, and then bam, you lay into your solo and you win over everybody in the room. And most importantly, the musicians and the ladies in the room. And, you know, there's a visceral thing. And if you haven't done it, then, you know, it's kind of introducing you from an experienced viewpoint of what you might suspect would happen, you know, when you get up on stage. So it's daunting up there under the lights. I'm going to tell you. Well, you did a great job of hitting those points. And then on page two or three here, you go into the story. You know, here's the story you're going to love. You may not have heard of Rudy. Rudy Parrish yet, but notorious guitar slingers worldwide go out of their way to hear him play. Billy Gibson is easy top hug Rudy after watching him tear up the room during one of those Rudy's many hours. Many tours with notorious punk ability Hank Williams, a third at Tide Prey. And you go into a story basically about your hero here. Talk about his stone-tone guitars being unveiled at the NAMM show and so forth. Stories are a big part of everything that you do. And, you know, that's an element that I've only used, you know, telling a story in the copy rather than a, you know, I'm doing financial stuff all the time. I'm talking about Bernanke at the Fed, you know, and why yes, lower interest rates. And I'm talking about macroeconomic trends or I'm talking about how brokers rip you off. But you have such an act for telling a story in a way that's so engaging that, you know, I'm hooked from, you know, the salutations beginning in the sales letter. But I notice in this one, and we're going to look at another one here in a second, but in this particular letter, the story copy really starts later on in the letter rather than leading with it. And that shows some flexibility I really like. Depending on the product, you know, you may go in with a story right off the top like one-legged golfer. Right. Or this story can be used later, but either way, they're wonderful engagement devices. You know, Clint, just to say something, you know, I do a lot of critiques of people's writing and they're all over the map. And one of the hardest things that people seem to get the idea of the story and the idea that they want to tell a story, and that's often part of the hook that they want to make work. But there has to be a seamless transition. The actual word is segue, which is a lost art in America because segue actually, you know, goes to radio DJs. You used to segue from one song to the other, so it was a smooth transition. There wasn't this jarring, you know, first you got a slow ballad, then you got some, you know, really fast song. They would actually set a mood and stay with it. And I remember that. I used to hang out with the DJs in college. And I was a big fan of FM radio back in the 60s. I was that kid hiding under the covers with my little transistor, listening to the DJs spin tunes. And when you do a story, it has to blend seamlessly into the sales pitch. So you can't like say, okay, I'm going to do the sales pitch, and I'm going to slap a story in here, and then I'm going to go back to the sales pitch. That's a jarring thing. This is all about how you would talk with the guy face to face, how you would seamlessly blend, you know, and it's almost like that breath of singing. It's like, oh wow, wow, I found the perfect Porsche engine for you Clayton. You only have to do a few things to be able to get it. It's over in Virginia. We're going to have to go over there and get it, you know. But let me tell you where this thing came from. You know, there's these two hillbillies that are living in West Virginia, and they stole a Porsche truck. They didn't know what they had. You know, and then you go into it, and it's all seamless. You know, you rattle your cage with the promise. You let them know. I mean, there's no sense not to let them know you're doing a sales with it, but you don't get into the specifics of the sale. You just let them know. This is an opportunity, dude. This is something, you know, I'm not just telling you a story because I like to, you know, rattle my teeth. I'm telling you this because this is important to the bottom line. The bottom line is, dude, your life is about to change. We can never forget that seconds before a product sees our sales stuff, whether it's in the mail or posted or in a magazine or something, they didn't know you existed. They didn't know the product was there in most cases, and their day did not include taking out 5, 10, 15 minutes to read your story. I mean, you are an interruption in their life, and that's where all the skepticism and all the distraction comes in and all those things. So you've got to get in there, and you have to deliver a payload that is strong enough to make him, essentially, take the phone off the hook. I mean, as a metaphor, he closes the door and the rest of his life, takes the phone off the hook, and indulges in his passion, which you are sharing with him. You know what? You actually do that in two ways that I can see. Probably more, but two very, very clear ways. Number one, you do it by telling the story, but you do it with entertainment value. You reward me for reading this copy by entertaining me. You know, there's the old thing, you know, when you pick up a sales letter, a clock starts ticking in your head, and if it's not moving ahead at a fast enough pace for you, if it's not delivering the information, at least compensating you for reading this, at some point during the process, before you get to the order form, you're going to put it down and walk away. And whenever I read your copy, and that, of course, when we talk to Arthur Johnson, the point that he made coming from Franklin Mints, the point that he made was that all of his promotions, everything he writes, he wants to read the copy on first draft and feel entertained as he goes through it. And your stories do that for me, as well as your tone and your energy and your copy. I think we all can learn a lot from that. In fact, going through these things has made me bound and determined that I am going to do a web-based promotion for Weiss that leads with a story, and probably also has a couple of other stories embedded in the copy. Hey, you're a storyteller. I don't know, all of us are. Because whenever we talk privately, we tell stories. I think I lucked out in that I had a family where I was the youngest by eight years. My sister was eight years older than me, my brother, ten years older. And sitting at the family dinner table, as a kid, I wasn't going to get a word in edge-wide unless I learned how to tell a riveting story. So there's a little bit of an advantage there in that I just grew up loving stories. I was in a story environment. I was astonished when I finally went to a friend's house each evening. They weren't trying to crack each other up and tell wild tales and stuff. I thought, what a boring way to eat. But all of us are, I tinder at storytellers. Everyone as you grow up. At my seminars, I give just one little homework assignment sometimes. I say, look, when you guys come here tomorrow morning, I want you to tell me a story about what happened to you between the time you closed the door of your hotel room and got to the conference room. Tell me a story. And most of them can't do it. And it's not because stuff didn't happen. When I actually dig, I find out, yeah, I was in the elevator with a, it looked like there was a woman from Russia there and she was drunk. Or things happened. Who did you pass in the hallway? I said, oh, it looked like there's a convention of Shriners in town because there were four guys with fezzes. I said, why did you say nothing happened to you? You didn't blank out and somebody carried you from your room to the conference room. All of this stuff happened and you were oblivious to it. You were in a white zombie state of not paying attention. When you learn to write and you write well, the world is your sandbox. Everything that happens to you becomes fodder for the story. And every story gets more and more interesting because as Arthur said, if you can crack yourself up, you're for sure going to crack up the people that you're writing to. That's right. We're looking at the page now that begins with Bullet, How to Comp Greasy Superfunky Organ Risk. You have a whole string of pearls here, a whole string of bullets. How do you approach writing fascinations or bullets? I invented something and I noticed that other people have ripped me off. I'm still used to being ripped off, but I invented this thing I called the two-fisted punch. And actually I got that. I invented the term, but it came from studying benzobingol bullets. Benzobingol to me remains and will probably never be surpassed. He's got the record like Barry Bond now has the lifetime home run record. Benzobingol knew how to write bullets that told the story, that did everything possible. When I started breaking it down, it took me a long time to figure out. He wasn't just writing how to bullets, how to double your investment in 10 years with absolutely no risk, kind of thing. But he wouldn't stop there. He would do what I call a parenthetical second punch. I use parentheses a lot, but he didn't necessarily do that. He would actually work it in. But it was like the one punch was a very good straightforward sentence that just often it was a how to or along that line. But then he'd come back with another uppercut that you weren't expecting that took another part of that benefit or another part of the feature or twisted it in a way that gave you another way to look at that wouldn't let you go. So it wasn't like how to double your wealth. Look at the second one here. An entire menu of fresh, new, specific licks you can throw into any solo that will turn heads and make other musicians swoop with melodic equity. Actually that should have been swoon, I believe. Yeah, that should have been swoon. So there's a title. And then parenthetically, not your same old tires cliched licks either. That's an important thing for a guitarist. Because whenever they get into, somebody says, let me show you some licks. There's a whole bag of tricks of really tired rock and roll and blues licks that every hacked guitarist out there knows. And if you want to get to the really fresh stuff, you have to move through that same old tire stuff. So it's like the one, two punch. You hit them once and then you come around and you kind of re-explain it another way. So that's the way I do that. And I really like the blind stuff. I like to throw in enough specifics to sound like you are delivering some real content. But I like to keep it blind enough that you've really got to, you know, what it does is start that itch. You kind of start the fire in your gut. One of the best testimonials I've ever gotten was, and I've gotten several of these, but people have written me to curse me because they said, dog, I'll tell you how I read your ad. I put it down. I was going to come back to it. I woke up at 3 a.m. and couldn't get back to sleep. Because something you said just kept rolling around in my head. And I couldn't get back to sleep so I got up and picked up the phone in order. And that's what you want to do. You want the immediate action, but you also want to get inside. You want to get inside your skin, inside your heart. Find that sweet spot of passion where you can actually, you know, their lives are now going to be incomplete until they have what you're describing. That's what the whole, and the fascination, I still call them bullets. I started doing a lot of bullets back working for the large mailers because I intuited it, and later I think I sort of proved it. You could never predict, no matter how good you were as a writer, you could never predict which bullet was going to trigger that action response. So you throw them all in. And sometimes that would be page after page after page of it. But if they're written well and it advances the story and there's some coherency to it and you're not just throwing them in because you're filling space. Every single bullet pays its way on the page. You could have page after page of bullets. And, you know, I do that because you never can tell really which one is going to flip the person. You know, it's easy to get somebody up on the fence to say, yeah, that's a pretty good product. I probably should own that sometime. And knocking them off the fence is where those bullets really come in and really nail it home because it paints that pretty picture in their heads. Just a minor question here. I noticed throughout this letter you used yellow highlighting. Is that something you do a lot? I started doing that with a Rodale piece. I believe it might have been the sex letter. I had always avoided color because of cost. Because I got beat one time out of having to control by a fraction of a percentage point. And, of course, you know, they take in cost of mailing when they figure out who wins the control. So I was always leery of using color. And then I found that I could actually use color. So I think I used a red headline and then went back to like a pink highlighting. I did that and it worked so well that I started paying more attention to the highlighting. I'm kind of backing off of highlighting now. What I'm going to do is start testing highlight because I've been asking a lot of people and nobody's really done a good hardcore test online about highlighting. I like it because when done right it helps scanners. So people that scan can pick up phrases that if you just wrote those phrases out separately with none of the other copy it would actually still tell the story. So it's like the major point. So it helps scanners. However, I'm getting so much resistance to it from people. You just say, God, it's so hard to read when you've got all this yellow highlighting. It's like a generational thing or something. I studied with a highlighter. You know, you highlight stuff in books and you're doing it for the reader. If he was smart he would highlight himself as he goes back or as he says, oh this is important, this is important, that kind of thing. So it's just a shortcut to – a lot of it is because I couldn't do – because of the margin problems or because online, because of the design problems. I couldn't do the other things I wanted to do which was handwriting. So I actually circled it with a – so it looks like I had gone in there and circled it before I left the letter to get out of my office and then wrote a little note in the margin. Yeah, and online. I mean you can do that now and people are doing that. But it just looks phony online because why would you write on your website? But back in the old direct mail days there were those – some of the best direct mail letters. I don't know if you remember but back in the glory days of the late 70s or early 80s they were using that classic stuff from the 40s and 50s where you would get a letter that was dog-eared and they purposely used bad typewriters with filled-in O's. You know, where the dirt was so full that every time they hit the O it was off-center and actually kind of smeared a little bit. The only other color they used was the signature. You know, they usually used a blue ink or something. And it really looked like they just dashed it out in their office and slapped it in the letter and sent it to you. Well, one time for a House File Campaign we pre-printed yellow-pad pages with the blue horizontal stripes and the red vertical stripes on the left column. Wow. And then we made the printer – after they were printed we put them in a paper vise and used a large bastard file to tear up the tops of the pages at 500 at a time so that they would look like they were torn out of a yellow pad. Right. And then they went to the folder and were folded and inserted. Yeah. Well, you know, when – a thing that Halberd taught is when we would send out tear sheets from newspapers we actually had them separate the pages with a saw. So there was a guy there with a big stack of newspapers printed two sides, one upside down and one right side up. And he would, you know, actually use a saw so that the edge was rough because when they cut it with their machinery it would look too clean. So you didn't get that sense that it was literally ripped out of today's paper and sent to you. I don't know if I've mentioned it to our members before, but one time we had a – it was for Blanchard and Company. We had a coin that Switzerland had – was about to introduce. And we had created it with them. It was a platinum coin. And so I did a promotion to my best customers where I sent them the blue line from the mailing. And there were intentional typos in the blue line with handwriting all over it saying, you know, correct this typo or remove this space and things like that. And then there was a cover letter that said, you know, please forgive me for being so unprofessional as to send you the press proof from the mailing that we're about to send out. But the fact is that the minute this hits the mail we're going to be sold out of this coin. And because you're one of our better customers I wanted you to see it first. And it's sold out, you know. That stuff was a lot of fun. I know, I miss it. Yeah, me too. About 10 times a day people around here hear me yelling, I hate the Internet. You know what it's just being, we are not the dinosaurs that people would like us to be. And a lot of these younger guys begrudgingly still admit that we still got the chops. And I'm still coming up and I know you are too because I follow your online stuff. We've kind of taken the opportunities that we see technologically and putting them through our devious direct mail train minds and coming out with tricks that other people are picking up. I'm kind of looking at the web as allowing me to be back in the early 50's when you really could start your own magazine and self-publish and do a lot of stuff. And that was exciting times. You know, I used to hang out with Joe Kosman. Remember Joe? Oh, absolutely. Who brought the spud gun and all that stuff? He was the guy who was in every comic book that we ever read. And it was just the true entrepreneurs. Back when nobody even knew what entrepreneur meant. You know, it was a word that no one uttered. And these were the guys that were really putting it on the line. And what an exciting life they led. The world was wide open. And to me, that's what the web brings. It's not so much that I'm in a hurry to find out what the model is that then we all have to follow. I'm just taking advantage of the fact that we're on the cutting edge of this thing. And it's this whole brave new world. And it's so wonderful, all these things we can do. So I'm in the video. And I did my first, you know, just me and the camera video. And, you know, I had a blast. This reminds me of, you know, I grew up in the 50s back when television was totally experimental. You know, and there wasn't that sense that, okay, this is how a series has to go. This is how a sitcom is. You know, laugh. They got to kind of laugh every minute and a half, or, you know, and all this stuff. And to get that formula stuff, I'm more interested in the breaking the rules and even making the rules. Let other people follow the rules that we create. You know, we're the guys that understand how to put those rules or those ideas or those concepts through the ringer to make sure that they got the best chance of working. And then, you know, when they work, it's great stuff. You know, I kind of feel like a fine art furniture maker must have felt when, you know, when they introduced power tools. Oh, yeah. You know, it's like before the Internet, we were working with chisels and, you know, hand files and things like that. And now we have a whole new toolbox, and it's learning how to use these new tools in the most effective ways that's the challenge. And it's interesting to me that it would happen to me at this point in my career. I don't plan to be doing this for another 50 years, you know. Right. And so the challenge for us now every single day is to sit around and think creatively, okay, how do we, you know, yeah, we can do web video. We can do a webcast. We can do audio on the web. We can do flash ads. We can do, you know, but how do those things come together to best effect in a campaign that will fully engage our prospects? And that's the challenge now. You know, like we were talking just a minute ago, during the direct mail days, we had it down pat. We had 20 or 30 years to learn all of these different, to try and experiment and to learn from each other. Right. To learn all of these different techniques and approaches and uses of direct mail. And I think we're still in the very early, early days of learning how to most effectively use the web, and it is a fascinating time. It can be frustrating, but it's fun. Yeah, it is. And what's interesting is the lack of rules. You know, the lack of law and order on the web is what, you know, makes people angry because it fills your inbox with spam. What's great about it for the entrepreneur, or even for the guy who's just looking for a way to communicate, is all of those lack of rules. You know, my blog is read by thousands of people that I'll never meet every day, all over the world. And, you know, that's publishing. Back before the web, that was a dream that, that wasn't even a dream that was possible. That wasn't something you would even think about. That I could write whatever I wanted to write, absolutely anything, in any language, in any way I wanted to do, have it posted within seconds, and then send an email out to thousands of people who were waiting to see what I had written, and then sit back and watch my Lexarake, you know, pop up, you know, for that day, or the next two days, and all of this, essentially for free. I mean, it's not, but for just, you know, no discernible outgo of money, as opposed to the old days when you had the mail, you know, a buck a package, you know, you had to print, you know, a buck fifty of a magazine, you know, and distribution, and all the problems of printing, and all of that, all gone. Yeah, that's wonderful. Well, listen, we're coming to the very close to the end of our hour. Sure. For everyone that's listening and with us on the web, I just want to point out there are two more packages here, and I think as you go through them, you'll notice all of the techniques that we've talked about, all the things that make John John, and instead of going with head gas, we've got head golf nut on this, on the next package here. We have a shorter headline. Yeah, Clayton, I just want to insert that these are true stories. This guy being dazed to live by surgeons, that was part of the sales detective work, and I found out that this was a true story. I mean, I found this out, they weren't going to use this. They didn't think it was worthwhile, and I said, are you kidding? You know, I could see the headline as soon as I found out. This guy had had this problem, I think, as a appendix burst, and he had some problems with the surgery, and the docs really did say, you better take care of things because you're not leaving the hospital. And he came back out and hit the longest drive of his life. When you find those stories, those stories won't be delivered to you on a platter, in most cases, in my experience. You've got to dig for them, and that's where the detective work comes in. But I just want to emphasize, these are true. I don't make this stuff up. You don't need to when you know how to dig. Well, and I think the point you were making earlier about being observant, it's one thing to go and hang out with golf nuts, or to go and sit down and talk to your client who invented this product. But it's being able to listen that's the art there. And to be able to dig and then hear a good headline, or know a good headline when you hear it. Rosenthal used to tell me all the time, we'd sit around and laugh about it, I'd ask him, how did you come up with that headline, take all your books on gold and throw them into the East River? And he said, I was just talking to a gold advisor for two or three hours until finally he said that. He said, I like to let other people write my headlines for me. And that piece mailed millions back at a time when nobody was mailing millions. So the skill is it recognizing when you hear it. I mean Dan was just a master at it. Absolutely. Okay, well we have some questions now if you could give us just a few more minutes. Absolutely. All right, cool. Let me scroll back up. Larry Meek has asked me a question I'll answer at the very end here. Okay, let's see here. Okay, John McDermott wants to know how do you think a shorter headline would work on the guitar piece, for example, if you had just gone with how to blow veteran guitarists clean off the stage as your main headline and then use some of the other benefits or some of that other copy maybe in a deck rather than having one big huge headline? Well, you know, I guess you could test. My clients actually have not tested that and he picked the interesting part of the headline as a possible standalone. That's certainly an option and I'll tell you, I go through those options when I write my 30 or 50 headlines, you know, either in my head or wake up the middle of the night and scribble and stuff down. I'm trying to reach that essential core of what I'm trying to say. But you've got to remember you're never presenting your stuff in a vacuum. It's not just your ad and nothing else going on in the guy's life. This is going to appear in a magazine. There's, you know, 500 other ads, you know, including all the classified ads and all these other stories and all these other things going on and things going on in his life and all this other stuff. So I think the question refers to the fact that probably, and I will agree with this, probably if you can get, if you can do what you need to do in a shorter headline, it's probably better because of the, there's less risk of cognitive dissonance when the guy is reading it. You know, it's like you read a few things, it's a fraction of a sentence and you kind of get this. But really when you look at this headline, I mean, I'm looking at it now. You can almost blur your eyes and sit back and not actually read it and just kind of, you know, kind of blink and let your eyes graze over the headline and words pop out, guitarists, soloing secrets, things like that. You've seen that test site where they take every single word in a sentence and mix up all the letters in between the first and the last letter of the words and astonishingly you can still read it. Yeah. There's something to that. There's something to the way we absorb information. So, you know, I'm not, I don't think people sit here and go, you know, go in their mind, you know, moving their lips on the astonishing soloing secrets. No, I think it's a, it's a mass. It's a, you know, they kind of just, you know, rip through it and it's very jarring. But that's why I want to get, you know, soloing secrets up top. That's why I want to do it. And by the time they get to that payoff line, that money line, how to blow veteran guitarists clean off the stage, you know, hopefully they're slowing down at that point. You know, so the absorption level is there's just, it's all backed up and yet they're getting to that and you don't need to absorb that money line. It's actually, so I guess it's like telling a joke. If I could, can't you just tell the joke by telling the punchline? And no. They say comedians sit around backstage and they have all their jokes numbered. Yeah, yeah. They just have, say, number three and everyone laughs. Yeah, yeah. And actually that's kind of true. Yeah, you know, watch that show, The Aristocrats. Have you seen that movie? Oh boy, rings bell. Okay, well The Aristocrats is about that horrible, filthy joke that only comedians knew about. They did a whole movie where they had every single comedian you've ever heard of and some you haven't heard of telling that joke and discussing the history of the joke and stuff. And it's one joke. There's not a lot of jokes and each comedian brings a different twist to it and a different way of looking at it. And if you've ever wanted, and I tell people, another homework assignment I tell people is to learn to tell a joke. One joke, you know, need more. I hate jokes because it kills conversation. I prefer wit and humor and insight. But learn to tell one joke because that will force you to tell a concise, short joke that doesn't lose the listener, does the setup correctly and delivers the punchline. By your first copy of Playboy ever, go to the jokes page, learn one joke, memorize it, learn it, and the way they present it is the way it should be told. And this will help you. This will help you understand how to be concise and deliver a punchline. That's good advice. Zachary Romero has a question and it gets back to getting inside your prospect's head. He says, let's say you take on a new client, a new market you've never written for before, you don't know this product at all. What steps would you take to get into your prospect's head? Well, actually, I did kind of talk about that at the start. I'm wondering if maybe he missed the first part of the call. But it is about getting down and talking with them. Well, actually, I will add to that. Often when I deal with a client, a new client, I will get what I call the company line from the main guy. And of course, Clayton, when you start dealing with a lot of different clients, you only want to deal with the guy that can write the check. You don't want to be dealing with an underling who's going to have to take whatever you say and turn around and talk to the guy that can write the check and give him his spin. You want to talk directly to the guy that's going to pay you. That's just a freelancing tactic that you absolutely must get to. But when you're doing your sales detective research, you want to get away from the company line as fast as possible. So I want to talk to the secretaries, the guys on the street, the guys who created the thing. And I want to hear the gossip. And I want to hear the stuff they're keeping from the top guy that half of the returns are because of one particular problem. So that's an obstacle that I'm going to confront and actually explain in the copy. That could actually save him on a lot of his returns. I want to find out what the guys who are doing either on the phone or face to face or the guys who are actually in the street trying to sell this thing, how they sell it. And it often has nothing to do with the company line. The guy who runs the company, the guy who has created the product, the guy who's dating this is, thinks it does one thing or several things. And often people buy it for reasons that have nothing to do with any of those things. You've got to get away from the company line. Otherwise, you really are a hooker of the lowest order. You are taking the memo that the boss sends out and turning it into a sales letter. And that's weak writing. You're just echoing something. You literally are like a UN translator, no original thought at all. And as a high-end copywriter, you've got to bring what you know about salesmanship to the table or otherwise you're just a transcriber. That's good. Okay, we've got Herron. He wants to know what are the secrets of selling subscriptions and continuity programs and having the customer stick for a long time? Do you have any thoughts on that? Yeah, of course. Have them fall in love with you. This all gets back to romance and stuff. I liken the level of a sale to a romantic thing. If you've got a $19 product or something, it's kind of like asking for a phone number from a stranger. It's not that much. If you're getting up to 99 bucks or high ticket items, it's asking for more commitment. It's asking for — and think about what you have to do as far as your own credibility and as far as making yourself look inviting. You may think, getting back to the romance metaphor that putting on a nice suit and driving a fancy car is all you need to do to have the girls come running, so to speak, as a metaphor for having customers come running. That's just not true. You have to go deeper. That may be your hook or that may be your foot in the door, so to speak, but you've got to be able to back the stuff up. That's just being a real person. Michael Larry is a real person, and I took his personality and kind of amplified it. When we chat, it's my voice. It's not his voice that's coming out here, but I am thinking about keeping it consistent. There's a consistent voice. There's a sense of shared passion. There's a sense of more to come. There's a sense of a relationship that has legs, essentially. That is going to last. That should last. At first, you might have to use tricks saying, and next month, we're going to deal with cliffhanger stuff. That always helps. I think that's a lost art. Clayton, I know that you do that extremely well in your newsletter. You're talking about what's coming up, laying the groundwork for how great it's going to be to just hang around a little bit longer. It's really about the same things you do to keep people in your life, either in a relationship, keep them a little longer at the party. The party seems to be winding down, but you've got clowns coming in half an hour. It's an interesting balance. I'd just like to throw in for Heron. It's an interesting balance. If your initial promotion over promises and therefore your product under delivers, there's really no amount of tap dancing you can do to keep people with you longer on a continuity program. You have to find a balance between hard hitting, but accurate representation of the product and the benefits on the front end, and then high quality delivery of the product. There is no other way to make a continuity program work. We're proud to say that in our Easy Writers Club, my average client that uses continuity programs, the first question I ask is, well, how long does a customer stick? And the answer is always eight months. In our Easy Writers Club, we don't even know what our average is yet because 99% of our monthly members are still with us after a year. And we're going strong. So it really has to do with that balance between the expectation and the actual delivery of the product. I think that has a lot to do with it. And Clayton, I would add too that once you get into that mindset of over-delivering honest value, it's actually easier than if you were running a scam. Because if you're not interested enough to really get in and to wake up in the morning and go, let's see what's going on with the forum today. Here's a mail from one of my students or one of the guys in my program. If your mind isn't buzzing with joy at the thought of continuous things, then you might be in the wrong business. And if you are in the right business and you're delivering this stuff, it should be a joy and it should be part of your own ongoing education that you're providing all this stuff. You're reevaluating long-held things. You and I just talked about copyrighting and stuff. I talk about this all the time, all day long. I never get tired of it because it's not something that I'd like as a hobby. This is my life. This is what I've been doing for a long time. You're going to stop talking. I could go on for another three hours and so could you. I'm not saying that we're not going to, but the thing is we love this stuff. We love it and that shows. John, I noticed and several of our people have asked the question about the pieces that we showed them today. None of these are advertorials. These are direct benefits in the headline from the word go. They send a message. I wrote this because I want to show you something. I know you've done advertorial type promotions in your career as well. Were these ads or sales letters? Which were they? Why did you decide to go straight for the juggler with a benefit? I'm not sure if I understand. Did I also write these as advertorials? Is that what the question is? Your headlines go straight to the juggler with a benefit. That's a very valid way of approaching a product. Most of the things that I've done have taken a different approach. That basically approached the person and said, hey look, here's some value added information. I'm going to bribe you to read this by telling you three stocks to buy now or four supplements that you can take that will get rid of your hameroids or whatever. But if that value added advertorial copy, I have one ready and quick answer to the question as to why you might have decided to go with direct benefit copy or as opposed to value added advertorial copy. That is the length of the piece. When you're doing advertorials, you have a product at price point and a margin on that product and a market that is best served with very, very long copy. 24 pages 8 and a half by 11 or 36 pages 8 and a half by 11. That amount of space gives you the freedom to take the advertorial approach. But if you're doing an 8 page sales letter or a full page magazine ad, you don't have that luxury. In many, if not most cases, straight benefit headline will often be the best way to go. I'd also like to point out that John's approach, if any of you have the one-legged golfer copy, John's approach is so engaging that he's delivering value to the reader of his promotions in a completely different way. I think this is an interesting insight. John is saying, read this and you'll be entertained. I'm going to tell you a story that's going to blow your mind and you're going to love every minute of it because you love golf. I'm going to tell you about how the one-legged golfer taught me to do 500-yard drives. Go ahead. There's that shared passion thing. Now these guitars and golf are high passion index markets. A lot of times the Goldbug market is a high passion market. But when you get away from day traders, when you get to just guys who are investing, it may not be the same kind of high passion element there. And health too, unless you have a specific problem, unless you have Hemorrhoids, for example. And that really triggers it. Otherwise you're going to be thinking, jeez, health is interesting to me and there are a few things that trigger this. But unless you have a list of people who match up perfectly to the three big benefits you have, like arthritis, you're almost never that clear when you're going in the mail, for example. It's people who have responded to other pieces that had arthritis, maybe as a big one. But you can't really predict that that was the thing that hooked them. So these are single issue pieces, golfing, play guitar. I mean, I have a lot of benefits, but it is really kind of a single issue thing. When you're dealing with investment or with health or things like that, it's a much broader stretch of categories unless you are just having an arthritis cure and that's all you're going to talk about. And I think you'd have trouble going for 36 pages on just one topic like that. So that's something to keep in mind too. The way you can frame the thing. I always think about the conversation you'd have. How long the piece is, you start the conversation and you're through writing, when you've made your point, you've made your pitch. And I don't know why mine come out at eight pages, but when I write for magazines, three manuscript pages of Microsoft Word equal one solid page of copy in a typical magazine. So that's three pages. It's hard to write in three pages. So these ads, by the way, run are often three pages long in these magazines. They also stand out that way. So you'll have a right-hand page and then a double truck spread. And they found that that has worked really, really well, but it's very expensive and you're rolling the dice a bit. And they also run one page ads, and that's when I have to really get in there with the editor and really kick it down. John, thanks so much. This has really been great. You know, one of the cool things about having a guy that talks as fast as you is you can deliver twice as much content in a normal suit. That's funny. Okay, Clayton. As I would. Cool being. Thank you, John. I really appreciate it. Thanks. Great class.
